feedback.text: I am excited to see the proposed protections being put in place by this law and these protections are necessary in order to protect consumers from unfair and deceptive practices. One request that I have is to add protections for video games to this act. As it stands today, right now, a consumer can purchase a video game under the assumption they can play the game for time immemorial (or at least until hardware dies) but then the publisher can announce at a moment's notice that said game can go offline and be rendered unplayable, or in some worse cases, games that are in someone's library that don't require an internet connection to be played AT ALL can be taken away and in nearly all cases, no refunds are issued. This is a terrible practice. If a publisher breaks into my house and takes a video game they published from me, that would be considered theft. Would these practices not be considered the same? In the culture of today's landscape legally speaking worldwide, this appears to not be the case, and so I would like to see a protection put into place that prevents game publishers and developers from taking away a purchaser's access to their game. I do believe that ownership of products is under threat. Decades ago, this was a given that anything you purchased was yours. Today in an increasing digital world, this concept is becoming more muddled and so it is clear that new laws need to be put into place to protect consumers from digital theft. Thank you for taking the time to read my comment and I look forward to seeing this act pass.
